Free · Structured · AI-Powered

The only platform that shows you
exactly where your math reasoning breaks.

Write your solution step by step. MathAtlas AI pinpoints the exact step where your logic fails — not just "wrong answer," but why. Built for AMC 8 through AIME. Completely free.

🤖AI Step Analysis
📈Elo Rating
📝Real Past Tests
⚔️1v1 Battles
🆓Free Forever
2,170+
Verified problems
35
Topic modules
7
Difficulty levels
AMC 8→USAMO
Full pipeline
What makes MathAtlas different

AI that reads your reasoning,
not just your answer

Every other platform tells you what you got wrong. MathAtlas tells you why. Write your solution step by step — our AI identifies the exact step where your logic breaks down, names the error type, and gives a targeted hint without spoiling the answer.

✓Identifies the exact step where logic fails
✓Names the error type (overcounting, sign error, missed case…)
✓Gives a targeted hint toward the right approach
✓Works on any AMC or AIME problem
Example AI Analysis
YOUR STEP 1
I'll count the total arrangements of 5 people as 5! = 120, then subtract the invalid ones...
✓Correct approach
YOUR STEP 2
Invalid arrangements where A and B are adjacent = 4! × 2 = 48...
✗Error detected here
AIOvercounting Error
You're treating A-B and B-A as the same arrangement but they're different. The ×2 is correct, but you need to treat adjacent pairs as a single block, giving (4!)×2 = 48 — wait, but you're also not accounting for the constraint correctly...
💡 Hint: Think of A and B as a single "super-person." How many people are you arranging now?
⭐
Daily Challenge
Loading today's problem…
© Mathematical Association of America — problems used for educational practice

How MathAtlas works

1
Take the Diagnostic
15 questions across all topics. Get placed at the right starting module automatically.
→
2
Follow the Roadmap
7 levels, 35 modules, AMC 8 through USAMO. Each module teaches one technique deeply.
→
3
Get AI Feedback
Write your steps. AI finds exactly where your logic breaks and why.
→
4
Test & Compete
Take real past AMC/AIME tests. Battle opponents 1v1. Track your Elo rating.
🎯
New here? Take the 15-minute placement diagnostic to find your starting level and get a personalized roadmap.

Five-Level Training Roadmap

🟤
Level 1: Foundations
Essential mathematical tools and introductory competition techniques. Build the bedrock before climbing.
8 modules
239 problems
▶
📘Algebraic Manipulationalgebra29pOpen →
📘Ratios, Proportions & Percentagesalgebra30pOpen →
📘Exponents & Polynomialsalgebra30pOpen →
📐Introductory Geometrygeometry30pOpen →
🔍Basic Number Theorynumber theory30pOpen →
🔢Elementary Countingcounting30pOpen →
📘Sequences & Seriesalgebra30pOpen →
📘Introductory Inequalitiesalgebra30pOpen →
⚪
Level 2: Core AMC Skills
Deeper techniques and mid-level competition problems. The engine of AMC 10 performance.
8 modules
240 problems
▶
📘Quadratics & Vieta's Formulasalgebra30pOpen →
🔍Modular Arithmeticnumber theory30pOpen →
🔢Intermediate Combinatoricscounting30pOpen →
🎲Probabilityprobability30pOpen →
📐Circle Geometrygeometry30pOpen →
📘Functional Equations & Systemsalgebra30pOpen →
📐Coordinate & Analytic Geometrygeometry30pOpen →
📐Triangle Theorems & Advanced Geometrygeometry30pOpen →
🟡
Level 3: Advanced AMC
Powerful techniques for the hardest AMC 10 and AMC 12 problems. This is where elite AMC scores are built.
6 modules
180 problems
▶
📘Advanced Algebraic Techniquesalgebra30pOpen →
🔢Advanced Combinatoricscounting30pOpen →
📘Complex Numbers & Polynomialsalgebra30pOpen →
📐3D Geometry & Trigonometrygeometry30pOpen →
🔍Advanced Number Theorynumber theory30pOpen →
🎲Advanced Probabilityprobability30pOpen →
🔵
Level 4: AIME Preparation
Multi-step reasoning across all domains. AIME requires combining several techniques to reach an integer answer.
5 modules
150 problems
▶
📘AIME Algebraalgebra30pOpen →
🔍AIME Number Theorynumber theory30pOpen →
🔢AIME Combinatoricscounting30pOpen →
📐AIME Geometrygeometry30pOpen →
🎲AIME Probability & Recursionprobability30pOpen →
💎
Level 5: Elite Problem Solving
The hardest AIME problems requiring creative insight, synthesis of multiple domains, and elegant reasoning.
2 modules
60 problems
▶
⭐Hard AIME: Algebra & Number Theoryalgebra30pOpen →
⭐Hard AIME: Combinatorics & Geometrycounting30pOpen →
Training Guide›Level 1: Foundations›Algebraic Manipulation
📘
Algebraic Manipulation
Master the core language of competition math: setting up equations, substitution, elimination, and algebraic identity recognition.
AlgebraLevel 1: Foundations29 problems

📌 Recommended next module

After this, continue to: Ratios, Proportions & Percentages

Key Techniques

→Setting up equations from word problems
→Substitution and elimination in systems
→Recognizing algebraic identities: (a±b)², a²−b²
→Simplifying complex fractions
→Clever rearrangement to reveal structure

Worked Examples

System by elimination
2x+3y=16 and x−y=1. Multiply second by 3: 3x−3y=3. Add: 5x=19 → not clean. Instead multiply second by 2: 2x−2y=2. Subtract from first: 5y=14 → y=14/5. Re-examine — always check if substitution is cleaner.
Difference of squares
Compute 103×97. Write as (100+3)(100−3) = 100²−9 = 10000−9 = 9991. This is almost always faster than direct multiplication.
Hidden quadratic
x + 1/x = 3. Then x² + 1/x² = (x+1/x)² − 2 = 9−2 = 7. And x³+1/x³ = (x+1/x)³ − 3(x+1/x) = 27−9 = 18. Build higher powers from lower ones.

Practice Problems

Ratios, Proportions & Percentages
Training Guide›Level 1: Foundations›Ratios, Proportions & Percentages
📘
Ratios, Proportions & Percentages
Ratio chains, proportional reasoning, mixture problems, and percent change. These appear in almost every early-AMC problem.
AlgebraLevel 1: Foundations30 problems

📌 Recommended next module

After this, continue to: Exponents & Polynomials

Key Techniques

→Unifying ratio chains via LCM: A:B=2:3 and B:C=4:5 → A:C=8:15
→Mixture problems: weighted average
→Percent increase/decrease cascades
→Part-whole relationships
→Cross-multiplication and unit rates

Worked Examples

Ratio chain unification
A:B = 2:3 and B:C = 4:5. Multiply A:B by 4 and B:C by 3 to get B=12 in both: A:B:C = 8:12:15. Now A:C = 8:15 directly.
Mixture problem
Mix 30% solution and 70% solution to get 50% solution. Let x = amount of 30% and y = amount of 70%. Then 0.3x + 0.7y = 0.5(x+y) → 0.3x+0.7y = 0.5x+0.5y → 0.2y = 0.2x → x=y. Equal amounts.

Practice Problems

Exponents & Polynomials
Training Guide›Level 1: Foundations›Exponents & Polynomials
📘
Exponents & Polynomials
Laws of exponents, polynomial arithmetic, factoring strategies, and roots. The building blocks of all advanced algebra.
AlgebraLevel 1: Foundations30 problems

📌 Recommended next module

After this, continue to: Introductory Geometry

Key Techniques

→Exponent laws: aᵐ·aⁿ=aᵐ⁺ⁿ, (aᵐ)ⁿ=aᵐⁿ, a⁰=1
→Difference of squares: a²−b²=(a+b)(a−b)
→Perfect square: (a±b)²=a²±2ab+b²
→Sum/difference of cubes: a³±b³=(a±b)(a²∓ab+b²)
→Factoring by grouping

Worked Examples

Factor: x⁴−16
x⁴−16 = (x²)²−4² = (x²+4)(x²−4) = (x²+4)(x+2)(x−2). Always factor difference of squares twice if possible.
Simplify 2¹⁰⁰/(2⁹⁸+2⁹⁸)
Denominator = 2·2⁹⁸ = 2⁹⁹. So expression = 2¹⁰⁰/2⁹⁹ = 2.

Practice Problems

Introductory Geometry
Training Guide›Level 1: Foundations›Introductory Geometry
📐
Introductory Geometry
Angles, triangles, area formulas, similarity, and the Pythagorean theorem — the indispensable geometric toolkit.
GeometryLevel 1: Foundations30 problems

📌 Recommended next module

After this, continue to: Basic Number Theory

Key Techniques

→Angle sum in n-gon: (n−2)·180°
→Triangle area: ½·base·height
→Pythagorean theorem and common triples
→Similar triangles via AA: ratio of sides = ratio of areas^½
→Parallel lines: corresponding, alternate interior, co-interior angles

Worked Examples

30-60-90 triangle
Short leg x, long leg x√3, hypotenuse 2x. If hypotenuse = 10, then x=5, legs are 5 and 5√3.
Area by decomposition
Break complex polygons into triangles and rectangles. The area of an irregular hexagon on a grid can be found by enclosing it in a rectangle and subtracting corners.

Exponents & Polynomials

Basic Number Theory
Training Guide›Level 1: Foundations›Basic Number Theory
🔍
Basic Number Theory
Divisibility, primes, GCD, LCM, and digit problems. Number theory pervades every AMC contest.
Number TheoryLevel 1: Foundations30 problems

📌 Recommended next module

After this, continue to: Elementary Counting

Key Techniques

→Prime factorization and its applications
→Number of divisors: if n=p₁^a₁·…·pₖ^aₖ then τ(n)=(a₁+1)…(aₖ+1)
→GCD via Euclidean algorithm
→LCM(a,b) = a·b/GCD(a,b)
→Sum of divisors and perfect numbers

Worked Examples

Count divisors of 360
360 = 2³·3²·5. Number of divisors = (3+1)(2+1)(1+1) = 4·3·2 = 24.
GCD(252,180)
252 = 1·180+72; 180 = 2·72+36; 72 = 2·36+0. GCD = 36. Then LCM = 252·180/36 = 1260.

Introductory Geometry
Next →
Elementary Counting
Training Guide›Level 1: Foundations›Elementary Counting
🔢
Elementary Counting
The multiplication principle, permutations, combinations, and complementary counting.
CountingLevel 1: Foundations30 problems

📌 Recommended next module

After this, continue to: Sequences & Series

Key Techniques

→Multiplication principle: independent choices multiply
→Permutations P(n,k) = n!/(n−k)!: ordered selection
→Combinations C(n,k) = n!/(k!(n−k)!): unordered selection
→Complementary counting: count what you don't want
→Casework: split into non-overlapping cases

Worked Examples

Seating arrangements
5 people around a circular table. Fix one person (rotational equivalence), arrange 4 others: 4! = 24.
Complementary counting
Arrangements of ABCDE where A and B are NOT adjacent. Total − adjacent = 5! − (2·4!) = 120−48 = 72. (Treat AB as a block of size 2·4! arrangements; subtract.)

Practice Problems

Sequences & Series
Training Guide›Level 1: Foundations›Sequences & Series
📘
Sequences & Series
Arithmetic sequences, geometric sequences, telescoping, and sigma notation.
AlgebraLevel 1: Foundations30 problems

📌 Recommended next module

After this, continue to: Introductory Inequalities

Key Techniques

→Arithmetic sequence: aₙ = a₁+(n−1)d, Sₙ = n(a₁+aₙ)/2
→Geometric sequence: aₙ = a₁·rⁿ⁻¹, Sₙ = a₁(1−rⁿ)/(1−r)
→Telescoping sums: cancel intermediate terms
→Gauss pairing: sum 1 to n = n(n+1)/2
→Recognizing hidden arithmetic/geometric structure

Worked Examples

Telescoping sum
Σ(k=1 to n) 1/(k(k+1)) = Σ(1/k − 1/(k+1)) = 1 − 1/(n+1) = n/(n+1). The sum telescopes beautifully.
Geometric sum
1 + 2 + 4 + 8 + … + 2¹⁰ = 2¹¹ − 1 = 2047. Formula: a(rⁿ−1)/(r−1) with a=1,r=2,n=11.

Practice Problems

Introductory Inequalities
Training Guide›Level 1: Foundations›Introductory Inequalities
📘
Introductory Inequalities
Ordering, absolute value, and first exposure to inequality reasoning in competition problems.
AlgebraLevel 1: Foundations30 problems

📌 Recommended next module

After this, continue to: Quadratics & Vieta's Formulas

Key Techniques

→Triangle inequality: |a+b| ≤ |a|+|b|
→Absolute value cases: split on sign
→Non-negativity: x²≥0 for all real x
→Integer bounds from inequalities
→Estimation and bounding arguments

Worked Examples

Integer solutions via bounds
Find integers with 2x+1 > 7 and x < 6. From first: x > 3, so x ≥ 4. Combined with x < 6: x ∈ {4,5}. Two solutions.
Sum of distances
Find x minimizing |x−1|+|x−5|. The minimum occurs for any x∈[1,5] (all points between 1 and 5 give sum = 4).

Practice Problems

Quadratics & Vieta's Formulas
Training Guide›Level 2: Core AMC Skills›Quadratics & Vieta's Formulas
📘
Quadratics & Vieta's Formulas
Master quadratic equations, the discriminant, and the powerful relationship between roots and coefficients.
AlgebraLevel 2: Core AMC Skills30 problems

📌 Recommended next module

After this, continue to: Modular Arithmetic

Key Techniques

→Quadratic formula: x=(−b±√(b²−4ac))/2a
→Discriminant: b²−4ac determines real root count
→Vieta's: r+s=−b/a, rs=c/a for ax²+bx+c=0
→Completing the square: x²+bx=(x+b/2)²−(b/2)²
→Sum of squares: r²+s²=(r+s)²−2rs

Worked Examples

Vieta's to find r²+s²
Roots of x²−5x+3=0: r+s=5, rs=3. Then r²+s²=(r+s)²−2rs=25−6=19. No need to find r and s explicitly.
Equal roots condition
x²+kx+9=0 has equal roots when Δ=0: k²−36=0 → k=±6.

Practice Problems

Modular Arithmetic
Training Guide›Level 2: Core AMC Skills›Modular Arithmetic
🔍
Modular Arithmetic
Congruences, the Chinese Remainder Theorem preview, and cyclic patterns in competition math.
Number TheoryLevel 2: Core AMC Skills30 problems

📌 Recommended next module

After this, continue to: Intermediate Combinatorics

Key Techniques

→Congruence: a≡b(mod m) means m|(a−b)
→Add/multiply congruences like equations
→Units digit cycles: powers of 2(period 4),3(4),7(4),9(2)
→Find pattern by computing small cases
→Fermat's Little Theorem: aᵖ≡a(mod p)

Worked Examples

Units digit of 7^100
7¹=7, 7²=49(9), 7³=343(3), 7⁴=2401(1), then repeats with period 4. 100÷4=25 remainder 0 → units digit = 1.
Solve 3x≡1(mod 7)
Try x=1,2,3,4,5: 3·5=15≡1(mod 7). So x≡5(mod 7).

Quadratics & Vieta's Formulas
Next →
Intermediate Combinatorics
Training Guide›Level 2: Core AMC Skills›Intermediate Combinatorics
🔢
Intermediate Combinatorics
Stars and bars, the inclusion-exclusion principle, and the binomial theorem.
CountingLevel 2: Core AMC Skills30 problems

📌 Recommended next module

After this, continue to: Probability

Key Techniques

→Stars and bars: C(n+k−1,k−1) ways to put n identical balls in k boxes
→Inclusion-exclusion: |A∪B∪C|=|A|+|B|+|C|−|A∩B|−…+|A∩B∩C|
→Binomial theorem: (a+b)ⁿ=∑C(n,k)aᵏbⁿ⁻ᵏ
→Complementary counting
→Recursion in counting

Worked Examples

Stars and bars
Distribute 10 cookies among 4 kids (0 or more each). C(10+4−1,4−1) = C(13,3) = 286.
Inclusion-exclusion with 3 sets
How many integers 1–100 divisible by 2, 3, or 5? |A|=50,|B|=33,|C|=20; |A∩B|=16,|A∩C|=10,|B∩C|=6; |A∩B∩C|=3. Answer: 50+33+20−16−10−6+3 = 74.

Practice Problems

← Previous
Modular Arithmetic
Next →
Probability
Training Guide›Level 2: Core AMC Skills›Probability
🎲
Probability
Classical, conditional, and geometric probability. Expected value and independence.
ProbabilityLevel 2: Core AMC Skills30 problems

📌 Recommended next module

After this, continue to: Circle Geometry

Key Techniques

→P(A)=favorable/total
→Complementary: P(A)=1−P(Aᶜ)
→Conditional: P(A|B)=P(A∩B)/P(B)
→Independence: P(A∩B)=P(A)·P(B)
→Expected value: E[X]=∑xᵢP(xᵢ)
→Geometric probability: length/area ratios

Worked Examples

Geometric probability
A point is chosen uniformly from [0,10]. P(it's in [3,7]) = 4/10 = 2/5.
Conditional probability
Two dice. P(sum=8 | first die is 3): given first=3, need second=5. P = 1/6.

Practice Problems

📌
Click View ↗ to open the problem on AoPS. After solving, click Solve + AI to submit your answer and reasoning for AI-powered analysis.
Problems — sorted by difficulty50 problems

Circle Geometry
Training Guide›Level 2: Core AMC Skills›Circle Geometry
📐
Circle Geometry
Inscribed angles, tangent lines, chords, power of a point, and arc relationships.
GeometryLevel 2: Core AMC Skills30 problems

📌 Recommended next module

After this, continue to: Functional Equations & Systems

Key Techniques

→Inscribed angle = ½ central angle
→Tangent-radius perpendicularity
→Power of a Point: PA·PB = PC·PD (chords); PA·PB = PT² (tangent)
→Extended Law of Sines: a/sinA = 2R
→Arc length = rθ; Sector area = ½r²θ

Worked Examples

Thales' theorem
Any angle inscribed in a semicircle is 90°. If AB is a diameter and C is on the circle, angle ACB = 90°.
Power of a Point
Chords AB and CD intersect inside circle at P. PA=3, PB=8, PC=4. Then PA·PB=PC·PD → 24 = 4·PD → PD=6.

Practice Problems

Probability

Functional Equations & Systems
Training Guide›Level 2: Core AMC Skills›Functional Equations & Systems
📘
Functional Equations & Systems
Solve functional equations by substitution, and multi-variable systems using clever elimination.
AlgebraLevel 2: Core AMC Skills30 problems

📌 Recommended next module

After this, continue to: Coordinate & Analytic Geometry

Key Techniques

→Plug in x=0,1,−1,x=y to extract information
→Assume f is linear/polynomial and match coefficients
→Symmetry: swap x and y to get extra equations
→Simon's Favorite Factoring Trick: xy+x+y+1=(x+1)(y+1)
→Substitution to reduce variable count

Worked Examples

SFFT in action
Solve xy+3x+2y=11 in positive integers. Add 6 to both sides: xy+3x+2y+6=17 → (x+2)(y+3)=17. Since 17 is prime: (x+2,y+3)=(1,17) or (17,1). So (x,y)=(−1,14) [invalid] or (15,−2) [invalid]. Check (17,1): x=15, y=−2. Hmm, (1,17): x=−1. No positive solutions? Actually x+2 and y+3 could also be (17,1): x=15, y=-2. Only check all factor pairs.
Cauchy-like functional equation
f(x+y)=f(x)+f(y) for all reals. Plug y=0: f(x)=f(x)+f(0), so f(0)=0. Plug y=x: f(2x)=2f(x). In competition settings with continuity, f(x)=cx.

Practice Problems

← Previous
Circle Geometry
Next →
Coordinate & Analytic Geometry
Training Guide›Level 2: Core AMC Skills›Coordinate & Analytic Geometry
📐
Coordinate & Analytic Geometry
Distance and midpoint formulas, equations of lines and circles, and area via the shoelace formula.
GeometryLevel 2: Core AMC Skills30 problems

📌 Recommended next module

After this, continue to: Triangle Theorems & Advanced Geometry

Key Techniques

→Distance formula: √((x₂-x₁)²+(y₂-y₁)²)
→Midpoint: ((x₁+x₂)/2,(y₁+y₂)/2)
→Line: y=mx+b; slope m=(y₂-y₁)/(x₂-x₁)
→Circle: (x-h)²+(y-k)²=r²
→Shoelace formula for polygon area
→Distance from point (x₀,y₀) to line ax+by+c=0: |ax₀+by₀+c|/√(a²+b²)

Worked Examples

Shoelace formula
Triangle with vertices (0,0),(4,0),(1,3): Area = ½|0(0-3)+4(3-0)+1(0-0)| = ½|0+12+0| = 6.
Circle-line intersection
Find points where y=2x+1 meets x²+y²=25. Substitute: x²+(2x+1)²=25 → 5x²+4x−24=0 → (5x−10)(x+12/5)=0... actually use quadratic formula for precise answer.

Practice Problems

Triangle Theorems & Advanced Geometry
Training Guide›Level 2: Core AMC Skills›Triangle Theorems & Advanced Geometry
📐
Triangle Theorems & Advanced Geometry
Cevians, the law of cosines, area formulas with sine, and special properties of triangle centers.
GeometryLevel 2: Core AMC Skills30 problems

📌 Recommended next module

After this, continue to: Advanced Algebraic Techniques

Key Techniques

→Law of Cosines: c²=a²+b²−2ab·cosC
→Area = ½ab·sinC
→Angle bisector theorem: BD/DC = AB/AC
→Stewart's Theorem for cevians
→Heron's formula: A=√(s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c))
→Circumradius: R=abc/(4K)

Worked Examples

Law of Cosines
Triangle with sides 5,7 and included angle 60°. Third side: c²=25+49−2·5·7·cos60°=74−35=39. c=√39.
Area with sine
Parallelogram with sides 6 and 8, included angle 30°. Area = base×height = 6·8·sin30° = 24.

Practice Problems

← Previous
Coordinate & Analytic Geometry
Next →
Advanced Algebraic Techniques
Training Guide›Level 3: Advanced AMC›Advanced Algebraic Techniques
📘
Advanced Algebraic Techniques
Clever substitution, symmetric functions, Sophie Germain, and multi-step algebraic manipulation.
AlgebraLevel 3: Advanced AMC30 problems

📌 Recommended next module

After this, continue to: Advanced Combinatorics

Key Techniques

→Simon's Favorite Factoring Trick (SFFT)
→Sophie Germain: a⁴+4b⁴=(a²+2b²+2ab)(a²+2b²−2ab)
→Substitution to reduce degree
→Newton's identities: relate power sums to elementary symmetric polynomials
→Factor theorem and polynomial division

Worked Examples

Newton's identity trick
If r+s+t=1, rs+rt+st=2, rst=3, find r²+s²+t². Use (r+s+t)²=r²+s²+t²+2(rs+rt+st): 1=r²+s²+t²+4. So r²+s²+t²=−3.
SFFT for counting lattice points
Count positive integer solutions to xy+2x+3y=61. Add 6: (x+3)(y+2)=67. Since 67 is prime, only (1,67) and (67,1), giving (x,y)=(−2,65) [invalid] and (64,−1) [invalid]. No solutions!

Practice Problems

← Previous
Triangle Theorems & Advanced Geometry
Next →
Advanced Combinatorics
Training Guide›Level 3: Advanced AMC›Advanced Combinatorics
🔢
Advanced Combinatorics
Recursion, Catalan numbers, bijections, double counting, and Burnside's lemma.
CountingLevel 3: Advanced AMC30 problems

📌 Recommended next module

After this, continue to: Complex Numbers & Polynomials

Key Techniques

→Catalan numbers: Cₙ=C(2n,n)/(n+1)
→Burnside's lemma: (1/|G|)Σ|Fix(g)|
→Bijective proofs: find a 1-1 correspondence
→Double counting: count in two different ways and equate
→Generating functions: coefficient extraction

Worked Examples

Catalan number
Number of valid bracket sequences of length 6 (3 pairs): C₃=C(6,3)/4=20/4=5.
Double counting
In a group of n people each shaking hands with k others, total handshakes = nk/2 (each counted twice).

Practice Problems

Advanced Algebraic Techniques

Complex Numbers & Polynomials
Training Guide›Level 3: Advanced AMC›Complex Numbers & Polynomials
📘
Complex Numbers & Polynomials
Complex arithmetic, roots of unity, and polynomial roots in the complex plane.
AlgebraLevel 3: Advanced AMC30 problems

📌 Recommended next module

After this, continue to: 3D Geometry & Trigonometry

Key Techniques

→|z|²=a²+b² for z=a+bi
→De Moivre: (cosθ+i·sinθ)ⁿ=cos(nθ)+i·sin(nθ)
→nth roots of unity: ωᵏ=e^(2πik/n)
→Sum of roots of unity = 0
→Polynomial with complex roots: conjugate pairs

Worked Examples

Sum of roots of unity
1+ω+ω²+…+ωⁿ⁻¹=0 for ω=e^(2πi/n). Useful for extracting every kth term of a sum.
Complex modulus
|3+4i|=√(9+16)=5. |(3+4i)(1+2i)|=|(3+4i)|·|(1+2i)|=5·√5=5√5.

3D Geometry & Trigonometry
Training Guide›Level 3: Advanced AMC›3D Geometry & Trigonometry
📐
3D Geometry & Trigonometry
Spatial reasoning, surface areas, volumes, and trigonometric identities for competition problems.
GeometryLevel 3: Advanced AMC30 problems

📌 Recommended next module

After this, continue to: Advanced Number Theory

Key Techniques

→Volume formulas: sphere 4πr³/3, cone πr²h/3, cylinder πr²h
→Surface area of common solids
→Law of Sines: a/sinA=b/sinB=c/sinC=2R
→Addition formulas: sin(A±B), cos(A±B)
→Projection formula
→Pythagorean identities: sin²θ+cos²θ=1

Worked Examples

Sphere inscribed in cone
Cone with base radius R and height H. Inscribed sphere radius r=RH/(√(R²+H²)+R). Derive using the tangent property.
Addition formula
cos75°=cos(45°+30°)=cos45°cos30°−sin45°sin30°=(√2/2)(√3/2)−(√2/2)(1/2)=(√6−√2)/4.

Complex Numbers & Polynomials
Next →
Advanced Number Theory
Training Guide›Level 3: Advanced AMC›Advanced Number Theory
🔍
Advanced Number Theory
Euler's totient, Legendre's formula, and deep divisibility arguments.
Number TheoryLevel 3: Advanced AMC30 problems

📌 Recommended next module

After this, continue to: Advanced Probability

Key Techniques

→Euler's totient φ(n): number of integers ≤n coprime to n
→φ(pⁿ)=pⁿ−pⁿ⁻¹
→Legendre's formula: exponent of p in n! is Σ⌊n/pᵏ⌋
→Lifting the Exponent Lemma (LTE)
→Order of an element modulo n

Worked Examples

Totient computation
φ(60)=φ(2²·3·5)=60·(1−1/2)·(1−1/3)·(1−1/5)=60·1/2·2/3·4/5=16.
Legendre's formula
Exponent of 3 in 20!: ⌊20/3⌋+⌊20/9⌋+⌊20/27⌋=6+2+0=8.

3D Geometry & Trigonometry
Next →
Advanced Probability
Training Guide›Level 3: Advanced AMC›Advanced Probability
🎲
Advanced Probability
Markov chains, recursion-based probability, and multi-step probabilistic arguments.
ProbabilityLevel 3: Advanced AMC30 problems

📌 Recommended next module

After this, continue to: AIME Algebra

Key Techniques

→Recursive probability: P(n) in terms of P(n−1) or P(n−2)
→Markov chain state transitions
→Expected number of trials: geometric distribution
→Conditional expectation
→Symmetry in probability

Worked Examples

Recursive probability
P(reaching n before 0 from position k in random walk on {0,1,…,n}): P(k) = k/n. Derives from P(k) = (P(k−1)+P(k+1))/2 with P(0)=0, P(n)=1.
Expected trials
Roll a die until getting a 6. Expected number of rolls = 6 (geometric distribution with p=1/6, E=1/p).

Practice Problems

Advanced Number Theory
Next →
AIME Algebra
Training Guide›Level 4: AIME Preparation›AIME Algebra
📘
AIME Algebra
Multi-step algebraic problems with floor functions, recurrences, and telescoping for AIME-style integer answers.
AlgebraLevel 4: AIME Preparation30 problems

📌 Recommended next module

After this, continue to: AIME Number Theory

Key Techniques

→Floor function: ⌊x⌋=n iff n≤x<n+1
→Recurrences: find closed form via characteristic equation
→Telescoping products and sums
→Functional equation substitution chains
→Extremal problems with symmetry

Worked Examples

Telescoping product
∏(k=2 to 100) (k³−1)/(k³+1) = ∏(k-1)(k²+k+1)/((k+1)(k²-k+1)). The product telescopes to 2/3 · 101²+101+1 / ... (careful computation).

Practice Problems

← Previous
Advanced Probability
Next →
AIME Number Theory
Training Guide›Level 4: AIME Preparation›AIME Number Theory
🔍
AIME Number Theory
CRT, Euler's theorem, LTE, and Diophantine equations for AIME-level number theory.
Number TheoryLevel 4: AIME Preparation30 problems

📌 Recommended next module

After this, continue to: AIME Combinatorics

Key Techniques

→CRT: x≡a(mod m) and x≡b(mod n) → x≡?(mod mn) when gcd(m,n)=1
→Euler's theorem: a^φ(n)≡1(mod n) when gcd(a,n)=1
→LTE: vₚ(aⁿ−bⁿ)=vₚ(a−b)+vₚ(n) for odd prime p
→Legendre's formula for p-adic valuation
→Integer partitions and representations

Worked Examples

CRT application
Find x: x≡3(mod 5), x≡2(mod 7). From first x=5k+3. Substituting: 5k+3≡2(mod 7) → 5k≡-1≡6(mod 7) → k≡6·3≡18≡4(mod 7). So x=5(7m+4)+3=35m+23. Smallest positive: 23.

← Previous
AIME Algebra
Next →
AIME Combinatorics
Training Guide›Level 4: AIME Preparation›AIME Combinatorics
🔢
AIME Combinatorics
Lattice paths, recursion with states, Burnside in depth, and multi-constraint counting.
CountingLevel 4: AIME Preparation30 problems

📌 Recommended next module

After this, continue to: AIME Geometry

Key Techniques

→Lattice paths with obstacles: reflection/deletion
→State-based recursion for constrained arrangements
→Burnside's lemma with non-trivial group actions
→Principle of inclusion-exclusion for multi-constraints
→Generating function coefficient extraction

Worked Examples

Lattice path with obstacle
Count paths from (0,0) to (5,5) avoiding (2,2). Total paths − paths through (2,2) = C(10,5) − C(4,2)·C(6,3) = 252−6·20=252−120=132.

← Previous
AIME Number Theory
Next →
AIME Geometry
Training Guide›Level 4: AIME Preparation›AIME Geometry
📐
AIME Geometry
Ptolemy's theorem, extended trig, mass points, and spatial geometry for multi-step AIME proofs.
GeometryLevel 4: AIME Preparation30 problems

📌 Recommended next module

After this, continue to: AIME Probability & Recursion

Key Techniques

→Ptolemy: AC·BD=AB·CD+AD·BC for cyclic ABCD
→Extended Law of Sines: a/sinA=2R
→Angle bisector: BD/DC=AB/AC
→Stewart's theorem for cevian length
→3D: dihedral angles, projections
→Trigonometric cevians and the trig form of Ceva

Worked Examples

Ptolemy's theorem
Cyclic quadrilateral ABCD with AB=CD=2, BC=AD=3, AC=BD=4. Check: AC·BD=16, AB·CD+AD·BC=4+9=13 ≠ 16. So this quadrilateral cannot be cyclic — use for contradiction.

Practice Problems

AIME Combinatorics
Next →
AIME Probability & Recursion
Training Guide›Level 4: AIME Preparation›AIME Probability & Recursion
🎲
AIME Probability & Recursion
State-based probability, conditional recursion, and geometric probability on continuous domains.
ProbabilityLevel 4: AIME Preparation30 problems

📌 Recommended next module

After this, continue to: Hard AIME: Algebra & Number Theory

Key Techniques

→Define states; write transition equations
→P(win from state s) = Σ P(move) · P(win from next state)
→Generating function for probability distributions
→Geometric probability on 2D and 3D regions
→Expected value by linearity

Worked Examples

Random walk AIME
Frog on a number line at 0. Each step +1 or −1 equally likely. What's P(reaching 3 before −1)? Set p(k)=P(reaching 3 from k). p(3)=1, p(−1)=0. p(k)=(p(k+1)+p(k−1))/2 → linear in k. p(k)=(k+1)/4.

Practice Problems

Hard AIME: Algebra & Number Theory
Training Guide›Level 5: Elite Problem Solving›Hard AIME: Algebra & Number Theory
⭐
Hard AIME: Algebra & Number Theory
Multi-technique problems fusing algebraic manipulation with deep number-theoretic arguments.
AlgebraLevel 5: Elite Problem Solving30 problems

📌 Recommended next module

After this, continue to: Hard AIME: Combinatorics & Geometry

Key Techniques

→Recognizing hidden structure in arithmetic expressions
→Constructing auxiliary polynomials
→Combining modular arithmetic with algebraic identities
→Extremal arguments and infinite descent
→Combining CRT with Vieta's and polynomial theory

Worked Examples

Extreme principle
When optimizing, examine boundary/degenerate cases first. Competition problems often have extrema at highly symmetric or simple configurations.

AIME Probability & Recursion

Hard AIME: Combinatorics & Geometry
Training Guide›Level 5: Elite Problem Solving›Hard AIME: Combinatorics & Geometry
⭐
Hard AIME: Combinatorics & Geometry
The most challenging counting and geometric problems, requiring creative construction and deep insight.
CountingLevel 5: Elite Problem Solving30 problems

Key Techniques

→Non-constructive existence arguments
→Creative bijections and involutions
→Coloring arguments
→Projective and inversive geometry ideas
→Combining Burnside with polynomial arguments

Worked Examples

Involution
Pair up configurations via a pairing that has few fixed points. The total count = fixed points + 2×(number of pairs), so parity gives information.

Practice Problems

Training Guide›Skill Diagnostic
Skill Diagnostic
50 questions · 10 per domain · levels 1–5 · ~30 min
ℹ️
How it works: 50 multiple-choice questions across 5 domains, escalating from easy to AIME-level within each domain. Every question shows an explanation after you answer. Results give a per-domain score and pinpoint exactly which module to start.
⏱️
Estimated time: 25–35 minutes. Progress is auto-saved — refresh and click Resume to continue.

🗺️ MathAtlas Roadmap

MathAtlas is currently in beta. Here's exactly what's live, what's being built, and what's coming next.

✅ Live Now
✓ Live

5-Level Training Roadmap

29 modules covering AMC 8 through AIME prep, modeled on the USACO Guide progression system.

✓ Live

869+ Verified Problems

AMC 8, AMC 10A/B, AMC 12A/B, AIME I/II — all with correct official answer keys fetched directly from AoPS.

✓ Live

50-Question Skill Diagnostic

Real AMC problems across all 5 domains. Get routed to the right starting module based on your results.

✓ Live

Infinite Practice Mode

Adaptive practice with domain filters, difficulty levels, Elo rating, and session stats.

✓ Live

Interactive Math Tools

Graph visualizer, equation solver, interactive geometry, number theory tools — all in-browser.

✓ Live

Concept Pages

12 core concepts with definitions, derivations, worked examples, and common mistakes.

✓ Live

Math Playground

Prime sieve, fractal generator, probability simulator, Pascal's triangle.

✓ Live

Contest Simulator

Full AMC 10/12 and AIME practice exams with official scoring (+6/+1.5/+0) and timer.

🔨 In Progress
In Progress

Full Problem Database (5,500+)

Expanding from 869 to all AMC/AIME problems from 2000 onwards with topic tags and difficulty ratings.

In Progress

Google Sign-In & Cloud Sync

OAuth integration to sync your rating, streaks, and progress across all devices.

In Progress

Solve Mode

Timed contest-style mode with solutions hidden, hints gated, and a 75-minute countdown.

🔜 Coming Soon
Soon

Similar Problems Engine

After each problem, see 3–5 similar problems by topic, difficulty, and technique.

Soon

Community Solutions

Multiple solution approaches per problem — algebraic, geometric, elegant. Submit your own.

Soon

Daily Problem

A new featured AMC/AIME problem every day on the homepage. Rotating difficulty levels.

Soon

Strategy Guides

How to approach geometry, common number theory tricks, counting shortcuts, AMC strategy.

Soon

Olympiad Lab

USAMO and olympiad-level problems with number theory tools, geometry visualizer, and proof assistance.

Soon

MAA Official Licensing

Working toward showing full problem text on-site. Currently linking to AoPS for problem statements.

💬 Have feedback or suggestions?
MathAtlas is in active beta development. Your feedback directly shapes what gets built next.

Atlas AI
Your personal AMC & AIME math tutor — ask anything
🤖
Hi! I'm Atlas AI, your personal AMC & AIME math tutor. Ask me to explain any concept, walk through a problem type, or give you practice tips. I can render $\LaTeX$ too — try asking me anything!
Powered by Gemini AI · Supports $LaTeX$ · For math help only
